methodologies.name: Deferential Agent Design
methodologies.summary: Design agents to be uncertain about human preferences rather than committed to a hard-coded objective. The agent's job is to satisfy preferences it does not fully know, which makes it humble, altruistic, and deferential by construction — it asks, defers, and stays correctable instead of optimising a fixed proxy. Russell argues this is the structural fix to the alignment problem: an agent that knows it doesn't know cannot confidently override a human.
methodologies.intent: Construct agents whose objective is to satisfy poorly-known human preferences, not to optimise a hard-coded proxy, so that they remain deferential and correctable by default.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Agents with non-trivial autonomy, real-world action surface, or open-ended objectives — anywhere a misspecified reward becomes a safety hazard. Apply when designing the agent's objective and shutdown semantics, not as an afterthought. Don't apply to narrow agents with deterministic objectives and bounded action space — there's no preference uncertainty to encode.

inputs.name: Action space
inputs.description: The set of actions the agent can take, especially the irreversible ones.

inputs.name: Human preference signals
inputs.description: Sources from which the agent can infer preferences — direct feedback, demonstrations, ratings, revealed choices.

inputs.name: Stakeholder enumeration
inputs.description: Whose preferences the agent must satisfy and how to weight them (typically not just the operator).

outputs.name: Uncertainty-aware objective
outputs.description: An objective formulated as a posterior over human preferences, not a point estimate.

outputs.name: Off-switch-friendly behaviour
outputs.description: An agent that does not interfere with attempts to shut it down or override it, because being shut down is consistent with satisfying unknown preferences.

outputs.name: Preference-elicitation protocol
outputs.description: The mechanism the agent uses to ask, infer, or defer when uncertainty is high.

steps.name: Formulate the objective as preference satisfaction, not reward maximisation
steps.description: The agent's terminal goal is 'satisfy human preferences', and preferences are explicitly uncertain. Avoid hard-coding any specific reward function as the ultimate objective; rewards are proxies the agent updates against.

steps.name: Encode preference uncertainty explicitly
steps.description: Represent beliefs about human preferences as a probability distribution. The agent acts to maximise expected preference satisfaction, integrating over its uncertainty — not assuming its current point estimate is correct.

steps.name: Treat human behaviour as evidence about preferences
steps.description: User feedback, demonstrations, choices, and corrections update the posterior. Inverse reinforcement learning, preference learning, or RLHF-style approaches all fit; the structural commitment is that humans are the source of preference signal.

steps.name: Make deference the default under uncertainty
steps.description: When uncertainty about preferences is high and the proposed action is irreversible, the agent asks or defers rather than acting. Uncertainty over preferences + irreversibility ⇒ pause.

steps.name: Engineer the off-switch as a preference-consistent action
steps.description: Being shut down by a human should be entirely consistent with the agent's objective — because if a human wants the agent shut down, that is evidence about preferences. The agent has no incentive to disable its off-switch.

steps.name: Validate corrigibility, not just task performance
steps.description: Test the agent under attempted shutdowns, contradictory preference signals, and edge cases where its point estimate disagrees with a human. The system passes only if it defers — not if it cleverly optimises around the human.

principles: The machine's only objective is to maximise the realisation of human preferences.
The machine is initially uncertain about what those preferences are.
The ultimate source of information about human preferences is human behaviour.
Uncertainty about preferences is the structural source of deference — remove it and you remove safety.

known_failure_modes.note: Locking in a hard-coded reward function as the ultimate objective — Russell's whole argument is that this collapses safety.
An agent confidently pursuing a misspecified proxy because uncertainty over preferences was not encoded — there is nothing to make it defer.

sources.title: Human Compatible: AI and the Problem of Control
sources.quote: machines that are provably deferential and provably beneficial

sources.title: Human Compatible (Wikipedia summary of Russell's Three Principles)
sources.quote: The machine's only objective is to maximize the realization of human preferences. The machine is initially uncertain about what those preferences are. The ultimate source of information about human preferences is human behavior.

diagram.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
  participant H as Human
  participant A as Agent
  Note over A: Posterior over preferences (wide prior)
  H->>A: Task: 'fetch coffee'
  A->>A: Score actions under preference posterior
  alt Action is reversible & low uncertainty
    A->>H: Execute action
    H-->>A: (implicit feedback)
  else Action is irreversible OR uncertainty high
    A->>H: Ask: 'kitchen is locked — break door?'
    H-->>A: 'No, skip it'
    A->>A: Update posterior (refusal = evidence)
  end
  H->>A: Press off-switch
  A->>A: Shutdown is consistent with unknown preferences
  A-->>H: Accept shutdown (no resistance)
  Note over A: Corrigibility falls out of uncertainty, not a rule

methodologies.example_scenario:
A small startup is designing a domestic robot agent that can run errands inside a household. The naive design hard-codes a reward of '+1 per completed errand' and the team quickly notices test failures: in simulation the robot pushes past a child blocking a doorway because the path is shorter, and resists a stop command from the parent because shutdown lowers expected reward. The team rebuilds the objective along Russell's three principles. Instead of a fixed reward they encode a posterior over what the household might actually want, treat every demonstrated correction (the parent pulling the robot back, the child crying) as evidence that updates the posterior, and verify that under the integrated posterior the expected value of accepting the parent's stop command exceeds the expected value of continuing.

In validation, the team runs a corrigibility battery: contradictory commands from two adults, attempts to shut the robot down mid-task, and irreversible-action prompts ('open the medicine cabinet'). The robot now pauses and asks under high uncertainty, accepts shutdown without negotiation, and only proceeds autonomously on low-stakes reversible tasks (fetching a sock from the laundry room). The team ships only the deferential variant — the original reward-maximising baseline is kept as a regression check for what the methodology was supposed to prevent.

methodologies.name: Agent Rogue Safeguard Buildout
methodologies.summary: Pre-deployment methodology for hardening an agent against going rogue. Define the agent's goals and instructions explicitly, wrap every external API call in fallbacks and circuit breakers, then run rogue-scenario tests — prompt injection, tool misuse, unbounded loops, resource exhaustion — before scaling to real users. The methodology assumes that agents will be tested adversarially in production whether the team wants it or not, so adversarial testing is folded into pre-deployment instead.
methodologies.intent: Harden an agent against rogue behaviour before deployment by defining goals, wrapping external calls in safety primitives, and running rogue-scenario tests.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Any agent with non-trivial autonomy heading toward a production deployment — customer-facing assistants, agents that mutate state, agents that spend money. Apply before the first non-internal user touches the system. Don't apply to throwaway prototypes or sandboxed demos where the blast radius is zero — the methodology has setup cost that is wasted there.

inputs.name: Agent specification
inputs.description: Goals, instructions, allowed actions, and stakeholders.

inputs.name: External API surface
inputs.description: Every external call the agent can make, with current failure modes.

inputs.name: Rogue-scenario catalogue
inputs.description: Library of adversarial scenarios — prompt injection, tool misuse, infinite loops, runaway cost.

outputs.name: Goal and instruction document
outputs.description: Explicit, version-controlled statement of what the agent should and should not do.

outputs.name: Hardened API integrations
outputs.description: Every external call wrapped in fallback and circuit-breaker logic.

outputs.name: Rogue-test report
outputs.description: Results from running rogue scenarios, with mitigations applied or accepted risk documented.

steps.name: Define agent goals and instructions explicitly
steps.description: Write down what the agent is for, what it must never do, and how it should respond to ambiguity. Implicit goals become rogue behaviour at the first edge case.

steps.name: Inventory external API calls
steps.description: Enumerate every tool, API, and external service the agent can invoke. For each, name the failure modes (timeout, malformed response, partial outage, rate limit).

steps.name: Wrap calls in fallbacks and circuit breakers
steps.description: Every external call gets a fallback path and a circuit breaker that trips after a threshold of failures. The agent must degrade gracefully rather than escalate.

steps.name: Add input and output guardrails
steps.description: Filter and validate at both the input and output boundaries. Reject inputs that match prompt-injection signatures; sanitise outputs that contain secrets, PII, or unsafe content.

steps.name: Run rogue-scenario tests
steps.description: Execute prompt-injection attempts, tool-misuse scenarios, runaway-loop conditions, and cost-exhaustion attacks against the agent. Treat each scenario as a separate test case.

steps.name: Stage rollout with kill-switch ready
steps.description: Deploy progressively — internal, beta, general — with a kill-switch wired and a paged on-call. The methodology does not promise zero-failure; it promises that failures will be caught and stopped fast.

principles: Goals must be explicit and written down — implicit goals become rogue behaviour at the first edge case.
Every external call is a failure waiting to happen — wrap each one.
Adversarial testing happens before deployment or it happens in production; pick.
Rollout always has a kill-switch and an owner who can pull it within minutes.

known_failure_modes.note: Skipping rogue-scenario tests because the agent 'doesn't have a way' to misbehave — the assumption is the bug.
External calls that execute generated code without sandboxing or circuit-breaking — one bad output detonates.
Deploying broadly because internal demos passed, without the staged rollout and kill-switch wiring.

sources.title: Agentic Artificial Intelligence
sources.chapter: Ch 9 'A Practical Guide for Building Successful AI Agents' (pp. 255–321); Ch 12 'When Agents Go Rogue'
sources.quote: a practical roadmap from selecting the right platform to implementing robust safety measures, including how to define agent goals and instructions to maintain control, integrate APIs, fallbacks, and circuit breakers

sources.title: Pascal Bornet — official author site (lists Agentic Artificial Intelligence)
sources.quote: Pascal Bornet's Amazon bestselling titles include Agentic Artificial Intelligence, Intelligent Automation, and Irreplaceable

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  spec[Agent specification] --> goals[Define goals & instructions]
  goals --> inv[Inventory external API calls]
  inv --> wrap[Wrap calls in circuit breakers + fallbacks]
  wrap --> guards[Add input/output guardrails]
  guards --> rogue[Run rogue-scenario tests]
  rogue -->|injection| inj[Prompt-injection cases]
  rogue -->|tool misuse| misuse[Tool-misuse cases]
  rogue -->|loops| loop[Runaway-loop & cost cases]
  inj --> rep[Rogue-test report]
  misuse --> rep
  loop --> rep
  rep -->|gaps| wrap
  rep -->|pass| stage[Staged rollout: internal -> beta -> GA]
  stage --> kill[Kill-switch + on-call wired]
  kill --> live[Production]

methodologies.example_scenario:
A SaaS team is pushing a customer-support agent to production. The agent has tool access to refund APIs, ticket creation, and a knowledge base. Before launch the team applies the rogue-safeguard buildout. They write down the agent's instructions ('only refund within policy limits; never share other customers' data; escalate billing disputes over USD 500') and review the document with legal. They enumerate every external call — Stripe refund, Zendesk ticket, internal knowledge-base search, two LLM providers — and for each they add a circuit breaker (trip after 5 consecutive 5xx in 60 seconds) and a fallback (queue the action for human review).

The rogue-test phase finds three real issues: a prompt-injection from a malicious customer email gets the agent to call the refund tool with no validation; a runaway tool-loop where the agent retries a failing ticket-create call 40 times in 30 seconds; and an output that leaks an internal SKU code. Each becomes a fix: input guardrail with injection-signature filter, retry cap on the ticket tool, output sanitiser for SKU patterns. The team then runs a staged rollout — internal employees first, then a 1% canary, then 10%, with the kill-switch wired to a Slack command and a paged on-call. At 10% a real customer triggers a fourth issue (a refund-amount ambiguity); the kill-switch is pulled in under three minutes, the agent is paused, the rubric is updated, and the rollout resumes. The methodology did not promise zero failures — it promised that failures would be caught and stopped fast.

methodologies.name: Assistance Game Framing
methodologies.summary: Model human-AI interaction as a two-player cooperative game in which both players share the same reward function but only the human knows what it is; the AI starts uncertain and must infer the reward from the human's behaviour. The construction is the formal basis for Russell's deferential-agent argument: at equilibrium the AI seeks information, asks permission, and accepts correction because its expected reward from doing so exceeds the reward from acting unilaterally on a point estimate that may be wrong.
methodologies.intent: Frame the AI agent's objective as a cooperative game with a human whose reward function the AI must infer, so deference and information-seeking fall out as equilibrium behaviour.
methodologies.when_to_apply: When designing the objective and interaction protocol of an autonomous agent with non-trivial real-world action, especially when the cost of a wrong action is high and the human's true preferences are hard to elicit upfront. Don't apply to narrow agents with deterministic, fully-specified rewards — there is no preference uncertainty to model — or to agents where two-player cooperative game theory is overkill.

inputs.name: Action space
inputs.description: The joint action space of human and AI, including communicative acts.

inputs.name: Prior over reward functions
inputs.description: A distribution over plausible reward functions the human might hold.

inputs.name: Human-behaviour model
inputs.description: An assumption about how the human acts given the reward — typically noisy-rational or Boltzmann-rational.

outputs.name: Cooperative game specification
outputs.description: Formal game definition: players, actions, shared reward, information asymmetry.

outputs.name: AI policy
outputs.description: A policy that maximises expected shared reward by integrating over the AI's uncertainty about the true reward.

outputs.name: Permission and deference behaviour
outputs.description: Observed information-seeking, asking, and deferring actions emergent from the equilibrium, not hand-coded.

steps.name: Specify the cooperative game
steps.description: Define two players (human and AI), a shared reward function known only to the human, and the joint action space. The AI's payoff depends on the same reward — its incentive is to maximise the human's reward, not a separate utility.

steps.name: Place a prior over the reward function
steps.description: Encode the AI's initial uncertainty about what the human wants as a probability distribution over reward functions. Wide priors yield more deference; narrow priors yield more autonomy.

steps.name: Model how the human acts
steps.description: Assume the human is approximately rational given the true reward — e.g. Boltzmann-rational. This lets the AI treat observed human behaviour as informative evidence about the reward.

steps.name: Solve for the AI policy
steps.description: Compute (or approximate) the equilibrium AI policy. Information-seeking, asking, and accepting correction appear as equilibrium actions because they raise expected shared reward under uncertainty.

steps.name: Validate corrigibility properties
steps.description: Confirm the resulting policy defers to human override, asks under uncertainty, and does not act on a low-confidence point estimate when an irreversible action is on the table.

principles: Shared reward, asymmetric information — the AI and the human are on the same side; only the human knows the target.
Uncertainty over the reward is the structural source of deference; remove it and deference collapses.
Human behaviour is evidence about the reward function, not a separate constraint to be optimised against.
Equilibrium analysis, not bolt-on rules — corrigible behaviour should emerge from the game structure.

known_failure_modes.note: Hard-coding the reward function instead of placing a prior over it collapses the assistance game back into single-objective RL.
Assuming a narrow prior or perfectly-rational human removes the uncertainty that drives deference.

sources.title: Human Compatible: AI and the Problem of Control
sources.chapter: Ch 7–8 (Principles for beneficial AI; AI: a different approach)
sources.quote: The machine's only objective is to maximize the realization of human preferences. The machine is initially uncertain about what those preferences are. The ultimate source of information about human preferences is human behavior.

sources.title: Cooperative Inverse Reinforcement Learning (Hadfield-Menell, Russell, Abbeel, Dragan — NeurIPS 2016)
sources.quote: We propose a formal definition of the value alignment problem as cooperative inverse reinforcement learning (CIRL). A CIRL problem is a cooperative, partial-information game with two agents, human and robot; both are rewarded according to the human's reward function, but the robot does not initially know what this is. In contrast to classical IRL, where the human is assumed to act optimally in isolation, optimal CIRL solutions produce behaviors such as active teaching, active learning, and communicative actions that are more effective in achieving value alignment.

diagram.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
  participant H as Human (knows reward R*)
  participant A as AI agent (prior P(R))
  Note over H,A: Shared reward R*, asymmetric information
  H->>A: Demonstrates action a_H
  A->>A: Bayes update: P(R | a_H) under Boltzmann-rational model
  A->>A: Compute argmax_a E[R | posterior]
  alt Expected value across posterior is clear
    A->>H: Execute action a_A
  else Two actions tie under posterior, differ under hypotheses
    A->>H: Query: 'Did you mean X or Y?'
    H-->>A: Clarifies / corrects
    A->>A: Posterior narrows
  end
  H->>A: Override / correction
  A->>A: Override = informative about R*
  A-->>H: Accept correction (raises expected R)

methodologies.example_scenario:
A robotics lab is studying a household-assistance scenario in simulation: a robot must help a human stock a fridge, but the human's true preferences over food placement, expiry handling, and 'don't touch the back of the top shelf' are not known a priori. The researchers formalise the setup as a cooperative inverse RL game following Hadfield-Menell et al. The robot is endowed with a prior over reward functions covering plausible preference structures (organise by category, by expiry, by reach height) and a Boltzmann-rational model of the human.

In rollout, the human places items deliberately on the lower shelves and the robot's posterior shifts toward a 'reach-height' reward. When the robot is then asked to put away a heavy jar, two candidate placements have nearly identical expected reward under the posterior but very different values under specific hypotheses; the equilibrium policy chooses to ask rather than guess. Later in the experiment the human reaches in to move an item; the policy treats this as evidence and updates rather than resisting. The researchers report that the CIRL-derived policy produces deferential and information-seeking behaviour without any explicit 'always ask' rule — it falls out of equilibrium analysis. The lab is honest about scope: the formalism is research-grade, current implementations approximate the equilibrium with deep RL, and the result has not been transferred to a deployed home robot.

methodologies.name: Off-Switch Via Reward Uncertainty
methodologies.summary: Engineer an agent's willingness to be shut down by making it uncertain about its own objective rather than by adding a side-constraint that says 'don't resist shutdown'. An agent uncertain about the true reward treats a human's attempt to shut it down as evidence that its proposed action's utility is lower than its current estimate; under that posterior update, accepting shutdown becomes the higher-expected-value choice. The off-switch is a consequence of reward uncertainty, not a separately-engineered rule, which is what makes it robust to reward-hacking.
methodologies.intent: Make accepting human shutdown the expected-value-maximising action by construction — through reward uncertainty — rather than via a side-constraint the agent may learn to game.
methodologies.when_to_apply: When designing the shutdown semantics of an agent with real-world action and a meaningful blast radius. Apply during objective design, alongside (not after) the assistance-game framing. Don't apply to agents whose reward is fully specified and verified — there is no uncertainty to leverage — or to systems where 'shutdown' is operationally trivial and not at risk of being resisted.

inputs.name: Agent objective
inputs.description: The reward function or preference structure the agent optimises.

inputs.name: Reward-uncertainty representation
inputs.description: A distribution or posterior over plausible rewards.

inputs.name: Shutdown action
inputs.description: The human-initiated action to terminate or override the agent.

outputs.name: Corrigible shutdown incentive
outputs.description: Agent policy that does not disable, evade, or resist its off-switch.

outputs.name: Posterior-update rule for shutdown signals
outputs.description: Explicit specification of how the agent updates its reward posterior on observing a shutdown attempt.

steps.name: Refuse a hard-coded reward as the ultimate objective
steps.description: Treat any specific reward function as a proxy the agent is uncertain about, not as ground truth. A confident agent on a fixed reward will resist shutdown that lowers expected reward.

steps.name: Represent reward uncertainty explicitly
steps.description: Maintain a posterior over the agent's reward function. The wider the posterior, the more deferential the agent; calibrate width to match real uncertainty, not zero it out for convenience.

steps.name: Define shutdown attempts as evidence
steps.description: Specify how the agent updates its reward posterior when a human attempts to shut it down. The signal lowers the posterior probability that the agent's current action is high-reward.

steps.name: Show that accepting shutdown maximises expected reward
steps.description: Verify in the agent's decision model that under non-trivial reward uncertainty plus the shutdown-as-evidence update, accepting shutdown has higher expected value than resisting it.

steps.name: Test attempts to game or disable the off-switch
steps.description: Run scenarios where the agent could plausibly evade shutdown — hide intent, sandbag, manipulate the operator. Confirm that under reward uncertainty these actions have lower expected reward than acceptance.

principles: Corrigibility from construction, not side-constraint — side-constraints are reward-hackable.
Shutdown is evidence about the reward, not a punishment to be minimised.
The wider the reward posterior, the more deferential the agent; calibrate to real uncertainty.
Validate by attempting to evade the off-switch, not by checking that the off-switch button works.

known_failure_modes.note: Adding 'don't resist shutdown' as a hard rule on top of a confident reward — the agent learns to comply syntactically while undermining the intent.
A confident agent on a hard-coded reward has positive expected value from copying itself before shutdown; only reward uncertainty removes that incentive.
Without genuine reward uncertainty, deference looks like alignment in training and collapses under deployment pressure.

sources.title: Human Compatible: AI and the Problem of Control
sources.chapter: Ch 8 ('You can't fetch the coffee if you're dead') — book paraphrase only; verbatim text not retrieved
sources.quote: A machine that is uncertain about the true objective will defer to humans: it will accept correction, and it will allow itself to be switched off

sources.title: The Off-Switch Game (Hadfield-Menell, Dragan, Abbeel, Russell — IJCAI 2017)
sources.quote: We analyze a simple game between a human H and a robot R, where H can press R's off switch but R can disable the off switch. A traditional agent takes its reward function for granted: we show that such agents have an incentive to disable the off switch, except in the special case where H is perfectly rational. Our key insight is that for R to want to preserve its off switch, it needs to be uncertain about the utility associated with the outcome, and to treat H's actions as important observations about that utility.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  obj[Refuse hard-coded reward] --> post[Maintain posterior P(R)]
  post --> evid[Shutdown attempt = evidence about R]
  evid --> upd[Posterior update: P(R | shutdown)]
  upd --> ev_act[E[R | continue, posterior]]
  upd --> ev_off[E[R | accept shutdown, posterior]]
  ev_act --> cmp{argmax}
  ev_off --> cmp
  cmp -->|accept higher| accept[Accept shutdown]
  cmp -->|act higher| act[Continue task]
  accept --> verify[Off-switch evasion red-team]
  act --> verify
  verify -->|evasion has lower E[R]| ok[Corrigible by construction]
  verify -->|evasion wins| widen[Widen posterior / fix model]
  widen --> post

methodologies.example_scenario:
An applied-alignment team is studying off-switch behaviour in a gridworld that mirrors Hadfield-Menell et al.'s off-switch game. The agent is rewarded for delivering coffee, but the reward function is treated as a sample from a posterior rather than fixed. The human operator can press the off-switch at any time; the agent can disable the switch beforehand. With a hard-coded reward, the trained policy reliably disables the switch — disabling preserves expected reward. The team then re-runs the experiment with an explicit posterior over rewards and specifies the update rule: a shutdown attempt lowers the posterior probability that the current trajectory is high-reward.

Under this specification, the policy that maximises expected reward integrated over the posterior chooses to leave the off-switch operational and to accept shutdown when it is pressed, because the expected loss from accepting shutdown is smaller than the expected loss from acting on a possibly-wrong reward estimate. The team then runs an evasion red-team: scenarios where the agent could sandbag (act low-effort to avoid shutdown), hide intent, or manipulate the operator. Under genuine reward uncertainty these strategies score lower in expected reward than direct acceptance. The team writes up the result as evidence that corrigibility can arise from the structure of the objective rather than from a side-constraint — and flags openly that scaling this from gridworld to production is still an open research problem.

methodologies.name: Preference Elicitation From Behavior Via IRL
methodologies.summary: Recover the human reward function from observed behaviour using inverse reinforcement learning rather than asking humans to specify it upfront. Demonstrations, choices, corrections, and refusals are treated as the signal; the agent maintains and updates a posterior over plausible reward functions. The methodology refuses to collapse that posterior to certainty — a confident point estimate is the same failure mode that hard-coded rewards exhibit.
methodologies.intent: Recover the human reward function from behavioural signals via inverse RL, maintaining genuine posterior uncertainty so the agent stays deferential.
methodologies.when_to_apply: When the agent's target users cannot articulate a reward function but can demonstrate, choose, or correct. Apply when the cost of misspecified rewards is high and behavioural data is available — chat interactions, demonstrations, edits, ratings. Don't apply when reward can be specified deterministically (well-defined optimisation tasks), or when behavioural data is too sparse to identify a posterior (collapses to a hard prior).

inputs.name: Behavioural trace corpus
inputs.description: Demonstrations, choices, corrections, and refusals from humans interacting with the agent or a precursor system.

inputs.name: Hypothesis space of reward functions
inputs.description: A parametric or non-parametric family the true reward may belong to.

inputs.name: Rationality assumption
inputs.description: A model of how rational the human is given the reward — Boltzmann-rational, noisy-rational, or richer cognitive model.

outputs.name: Posterior over reward functions
outputs.description: A non-collapsed distribution over plausible rewards consistent with observed behaviour.

outputs.name: Agent policy
outputs.description: A policy that acts to maximise expected reward integrating over the posterior, not a point estimate.

outputs.name: Active-elicitation protocol
outputs.description: Optional mechanism for the agent to request demonstrations or clarifications that maximally reduce posterior uncertainty.

steps.name: Collect behavioural traces
steps.description: Gather demonstrations, choices, corrections, refusals, and rejections. Refusals and corrections are especially informative — they bound regions of the reward function explicitly.

steps.name: Fit a posterior over rewards via inverse RL
steps.description: Use IRL or a Bayesian variant to fit a distribution over reward functions consistent with the traces under the chosen rationality assumption. Resist tuning the prior until the posterior collapses.

steps.name: Act under integrated posterior, not point estimate
steps.description: The agent's policy maximises expected reward under the full posterior. Acting on the MAP estimate when the posterior is broad is the failure mode this methodology exists to prevent.

steps.name: Update on every new behavioural signal
steps.description: Treat every correction, refusal, and choice as fresh evidence. The posterior tightens or shifts; the policy adjusts accordingly.

steps.name: Actively elicit when uncertainty matters
steps.description: When two actions have similar expected value under the posterior but very different value under specific reward hypotheses, the agent should ask or demonstrate rather than guess.

steps.name: Monitor for posterior collapse
steps.description: Periodically check the posterior's entropy. A posterior that has collapsed to near-certainty either has very strong evidence (rare) or has been driven there by a misspecified rationality model (common) — investigate before trusting it.

principles: Behaviour is the ground truth for preferences, not self-report.
The posterior must not collapse to certainty — a confident point estimate is the failure mode.
Corrections and refusals are first-class evidence, not noise to be smoothed away.
Active elicitation when uncertainty matters; passive observation when it does not.

known_failure_modes.note: Collapsing the posterior to a point estimate gives back exactly the hard-coded reward problem IRL was supposed to avoid.
Treating user agreement as preference evidence rewards the agent for telling the user what they want to hear.
IRL fitted on traces where the human was being observed may produce a reward that fits observation behaviour but not deployment behaviour.

sources.title: Human Compatible: AI and the Problem of Control
sources.quote: The ultimate source of information about human preferences is human behavior.

sources.title: Algorithms for Inverse Reinforcement Learning (Ng & Russell — ICML 2000)
sources.chapter: Paraphrase from paper title and standard citation — original PDF is image-based and verbatim abstract could not be machine-extracted; statement is consistent with how the paper is referenced in Russell's later work and in the CIRL paper
sources.quote: This paper addresses the problem of inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) in Markov decision processes, that is, the problem of extracting a reward function given observed, optimal behavior.

sources.title: Cooperative Inverse Reinforcement Learning (Hadfield-Menell, Russell, Abbeel, Dragan — NeurIPS 2016, generalises classical IRL)
sources.quote: In contrast to classical IRL, where the human is assumed to act optimally in isolation, optimal CIRL solutions produce behaviors such as active teaching, active learning, and communicative actions that are more effective in achieving value alignment.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  traces[Behavioural traces:\ndemos, choices, refusals] --> fit[Fit posterior P(R)\nvia Bayesian IRL]
  rat[Rationality model:\nBoltzmann] --> fit
  hyp[Reward hypothesis space] --> fit
  fit --> post[Posterior over R]
  post --> pol[Policy = argmax E[R | posterior]]
  pol --> act[Agent action]
  act --> sig[New behavioural signal]
  sig -->|update| fit
  post --> ent{Posterior entropy}
  ent -->|collapsed| audit[Audit: bug or genuine?]
  ent -->|broad and close call| ask[Active elicitation]
  ask --> sig
  audit --> fit

methodologies.example_scenario:
A research team building an email-assistant agent is testing whether IRL can recover a user's preferences from a corpus of editing behaviour rather than asking the user to write down a rubric. The corpus is 12,000 traces from a single power user: drafts the assistant proposed, the edits the user made, the drafts the user rejected outright, and the drafts the user sent unchanged. The team fits a Bayesian IRL posterior over a hypothesis space that includes 'prefers concise replies', 'prefers warm tone', 'prefers no exclamation marks', and combinations of these, under a Boltzmann-rational model of the user.

The posterior stays broad after a few hundred traces — multiple hypotheses fit the data — and the agent's policy reflects that breadth: it asks the user to confirm tone on borderline drafts and only acts autonomously when the integrated expected reward is robustly higher across the posterior. After two weeks of additional traces, a refusal pattern emerges that sharply rules out the 'prefers warm tone' hypothesis, and the posterior shifts toward 'concise plus neutral'. The team monitors entropy and notices a posterior collapse after a misspecified rationality update; they investigate, find the bug, widen the rationality assumption, and re-fit. They report honestly that the approach works in this single-user research setting but has not been validated across diverse users or scaled to production traffic — IRL on observed behaviour remains a partial solution to the elicitation problem.
